title: Dog Eye Swollen Shut: Causes, Urgency, and Triage Advice
meta_desc: Is your dog's eye swollen shut? Learn about common causes, when to seek urgent veterinary care, and how a photo helps triage your pet's eye health.
content_html:
TL;DR: A dog's eye swollen shut indicates significant pain or inflammation and requires a veterinary exam within 12–24 hours to prevent permanent damage. Seek immediate emergency care if the eye is bulging, bleeding, or if your dog is in extreme distress.

Why is my dog's eye swollen shut?

When a dog's eye is swollen shut, it is usually a sign of significant inflammation or pain. This condition can stem from several issues, including:

  • Blunt force trauma
  • Environmental allergies
  • A foreign object (like a grass seed) trapped under the lid
  • A corneal ulcer
  • An infection or an abscess behind the eye

Because the eye is a highly sensitive organ, swelling is the body's natural way of protecting it from further irritation.

Is a swollen eye a medical emergency for my dog?

Urgency Level: Medium. While a swollen eye may not always be life-threatening, it is a time-sensitive medical concern because eye conditions can deteriorate rapidly. You should aim to have your dog seen by a veterinarian within 12 to 24 hours. Seek emergency care immediately if you notice:

  • The eye appears to be bulging or protruding
  • Visible bleeding from the eye
  • Extreme pain or distress
  • Permanent vision loss risks

How does a photo help my veterinarian triage my dog?

Taking a clear, well-lit photo of your dog's eye can be a vital tool for your veterinary triage team. A high-quality image allows the professional to assess the following before you even arrive at the clinic:

  • The color of the conjunctiva
  • The type of discharge present
  • The severity of the swelling
  • Prioritization of your pet’s case
  • Preparation of diagnostic tools, such as fluorescein stain or tonometry

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Ophthalmic emergencies require rapid diagnosis and aggressive therapy to maintain vision. Traumatic proptosis, often from blunt trauma, results in globe luxation and eyelid spasms preventing retraction; secondary orbital hemorrhage and swelling exacerbate the condition. Prognosis depends on pupil size and reflexes, duration of exposure, globe or orbital damage, breed (brachycephalics are predisposed), and systemic trauma. Corneal foreign bodies can cause blepharospasm, tearing, and iridocyclitis. Swollen eyelids may impair the blink response, necessitating a temporary tarsorrhaphy to protect the cornea, along with topical antibiotics and corticosteroids, and systemic antibiotics and NSAIDs postoperatively.

Chapter: Ophthalmology, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1696)

faqs.question: Can I use human eye drops for my dog's swelling?
faqs.answer: No, you should never use human medications or over-the-counter eye drops on a dog without veterinary approval. Many human drops contain ingredients that can be harmful or even cause the eye to worsen if a corneal ulcer is present.

faqs.question: Should I put a cone on my dog if their eye is swollen?
faqs.answer: Yes, placing an Elizabethan collar (cone) on your dog is a highly recommended first-step. This prevents them from pawing or rubbing the eye, which can cause secondary trauma or deep scratches on the cornea.

faqs.question: How can I safely clean around the eye?
faqs.answer: You can gently wipe away discharge from the fur around the eye using a soft cloth dampened with lukewarm water. Avoid touching the eyeball itself and do not use soaps or detergents.
